Added tanwin alif (ـًا) at the end to show this is any thing (not specific) and is being acted upon. This word is what is being affected by تَظْلِم (diminish/wrong) in the phrase 'did not diminish anything from it'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Adding shadda to middle radical creates intensive/causative meaning

steps.m: and he made (something) burst forth abundantly
steps.n: Adding و prefix to connect with previous statement

steps.m: and we made (something) burst forth abundantly (referring to نَهَرًا/river)
steps.n: Added نَا to show 'we' did the action. The verb is acting on نَهَرًا (river) which appears later in the ayah.

steps.m: to pass through or between things

steps.n: Changes from basic meaning to plural noun form
Changed the alif to an alif with overbar (ٰ) - this is just a writing variation that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: between them both (referring to the two gardens)
steps.n: Added هُمَا at the end to show this means 'between them both', referring to the two gardens mentioned earlier in the verse
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun

steps.m: a river (object of فَجَّرْنَا/we caused to gush forth)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb فَجَّرْنَا (we caused to gush forth) in this ayah
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'
Added هُ with a prolongation sign (ۥ) at the end to show this means 'him'. This refers to the owner of the gardens who is speaking to his companion.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic noun form
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject and is indefinite (like saying 'a fruit' instead of 'the fruit')
Added fatha vowels to make it past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in a past tense verb

steps.m: so he said (to صَاحِب, his companion)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of what came before. He is speaking to his companion (صَاحِب) mentioned in the next part of the verse.

steps.m: to accompany or be a close friend

steps.n: Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to make it mean 'someone who accompanies'
Added لِ at the beginning to show direction or purpose - like saying 'to' in English

steps.m: to his companion (who he speaks to)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end to show it belongs to him. This person is being spoken to in the story.

This is a personal pronoun meaning 'he' that refers back to the owner of the garden who is speaking to his companion. It acts as the subject of the verb يُحَاوِرُ (he converses/dialogues) that follows.
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this clause to what came before. The pronoun هو refers to the garden owner who is speaking to his companion.

steps.m: to converse back and forth, to dialogue

steps.n: Changed from root letters to past tense verb meaning 'he conversed'
Adding alif shows this is about two people talking to each other

steps.m: he is having a conversation with (someone)
steps.n: Changes from past tense (he had) to present tense (he is having)

steps.m: he is having a conversation with him
steps.n: Adding هُ makes it 'with him' - like adding 'him' in English
This is a standalone personal pronoun meaning 'I'. It has no root since it's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic.
Added a small superscript alif (۠) which is a writing style variation that doesn't change the meaning. In this ayah, the speaker is boasting that he has more wealth than his companion.
Changed from root to past tense verb meaning 'became a lot/many'

Added أَ at the beginning and ُ at the end to make it mean 'more'. This form is used to compare two things, showing one has more of a quality than the other. Here it's comparing wealth, saying 'I have more wealth than you.'
This is a preposition meaning 'from' that connects to the previous verb يُحَاوِرُ (he converses with)

steps.m: from you (being addressed by the wealthy owner in his boast)
steps.n: Added كَ to show this means 'you'. The wealthy garden owner is speaking to his companion, boasting that he has more wealth than him.
Adding the fatha vowel mark helps create the basic noun form
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being compared after أَكْثَرُ (more than) in the phrase 'I have more wealth than you'
Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
Creates comparative form meaning 'more/most mighty'. In this ayah, the speaker is comparing himself to his companion (صَاحِبِهِ) in terms of might/power.
Adds conjunction prefix meaning 'and'

steps.m: to go forth as a group

steps.n: Adding the basic past tense vowel pattern
The word changed from a past tense verb (he went forth) to a noun meaning 'number/group of people' by adding ًا at the end. It tells us HOW someone is mightier - by having more people
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is the subject of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'

steps.m: to cover or hide (related to how gardens are enclosed spaces)

steps.n: Root letters got short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make a noun meaning 'hidden place'
Doubling the middle letter (ن) makes the meaning stronger
Adding ة changes it from a general hidden/covered place to mean one specific garden (like Paradise)
Added هُ to show the garden belongs to him

steps.m: he (referring to the owner of the garden who is speaking)
steps.n: This is a pronoun that means 'he' - in this ayah it refers back to the owner of the garden who entered it and started speaking

steps.m: and he (referring to the owner of the garden who is speaking)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects his state (being unjust to himself) with what follows (what he says)

steps.m: to do wrong or harm to someone/something

steps.n: Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who does the action. This describes the person (هُوَ meaning 'he') in this ayah.
The double-damma (ٌ) shows this is talking about any wrongdoer in general, not a specific one
Added لِّ (with shadda) at beginning to mean 'to/for' with emphasis
Added attached pronoun هِۦ to indicate possession ('his'). The ۦ mark shows this is a long vowel. The word نَفْس (self) is possessed by ظَالِمٌ (wrongdoer) in this phrase.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the basic form before the weak letter changes.
The middle letter و changed to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic that makes the word easier to pronounce.

steps.m: what (introducing a negative statement with أَظُنُّ)
steps.n: This is an interrogative particle that means 'what' and works with أَظُنُّ (I think) to express 'I do not think'

steps.m: what (introducing a negative statement with أَظُنُّ)
steps.n: The alif is written with a madda mark (آ) to show a longer pronunciation. This is just about how we write it and doesn't change what it means. Together with أَظُنُّ it forms the phrase 'I do not think'

steps.m: to think or believe something without being completely sure

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to damma and combined the double ن into one letter with shadda to make the present tense base

steps.m: I think (in response to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action. This word comes after قَالَ (he said) to show what someone is saying.
This is a particle that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects 'I don't think' (أَظُنُّ) with 'will perish' (تَبِيدَ), showing what the person doesn't think will happen.
Connected the root letters to form the basic past tense verb
ت at start shows it's about a female subject in present tense
This is the basic form meaning 'this' or 'here'
Added ذا to make it more specific for pointing to things
Changed ending to هِ to make it feminine, like changing 'this' to 'this one' for feminine things
Added pronunciation marks ۦٓ to show how to say it correctly when stopping at this word
Connected the letters and added fatha marks to create the basic noun form

steps.m: forever (describing how long he thought his garden would last)
steps.n: Added an ending -an sound (shown by two dots above) to make it mean 'forever' as a description
This is a basic particle used to negate things or ask questions

steps.m: and not/what (connects this negation to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it

steps.m: and not/what (connects this negation to previous statement)
steps.n: Added vowel mark on م and lengthened the final alif. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changed vowels to damma and doubled the middle letter (with shadda) to make it 'I think' in present tense
Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' am doing the action. In this ayah, the speaker is thinking about ٱلسَّاعَةَ (the Hour/Day of Judgment).
The و changes to ا because it comes after a fatha
Adding ة makes it into a specific instance or unit of time

steps.m: the Hour (referring to the Day of Judgment)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific. The س gets doubled (shadda) because it's a 'sun letter' that combines with the ل of ال. In this context, it specifically refers to the Day of Judgment.

steps.m: the Hour (object of أَظُنُّ - I think)
steps.n: The ال changes to ٱل for easier pronunciation. The final fatha shows this word is the object of the verb أَظُنُّ (I think).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changes from verb (to stand) to noun (one who stands)
Added ة at the end to make it feminine, and lengthened the ا (alif) to آ for pronunciation
Added -an ending to show this word describes 'the Hour' (الساعة) as an adjective
This is the basic conditional particle that shows something depends on something else
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'indeed' or 'truly'
The إِ changes to ئِ when it comes after لَ - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone rather than them doing it
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' was the one being returned
Added a doubling mark on the middle letter to show extra emphasis
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows direction or destination

The ىٰ (alif maqsurah) is added at the end as a writing convention. It doesn't change the meaning. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'I am returned' (رُّدِدتُّ) with 'my Lord' (رَبِّى) to show where he would be returned to.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or develop something to completion

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
The word now shows it belongs to or connects with something after it (like 'Lord of...')
Added ى at the end to show this is 'my' Lord. This word is the subject of لَأَجِدَنَّ (I will surely find) in this ayah.

steps.m: to find, discover, or come across something

steps.n: Changed to first person present tense by adding أَ at start and ِ in middle. The و drops because it's a weak letter at the start.
Added لَ at start to show strong emphasis and future certainty

steps.m: surely I will definitely find (with absolute certainty)
steps.n: Added نَّ at the end to show complete certainty about the future action. This double emphasis (لَ...نَّ) shows the speaker's absolute conviction.
Changes from verb 'to be good' into noun meaning 'good/better'

steps.m: something better (object of لَأَجِدَنَّ - I will surely find)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is the thing being found in the phrase 'I will surely find something better'
This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from or comparing things
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after another word ending in ن or م, making the م sound stronger

steps.m: from it/her (referring to the garden mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هَا which refers back to something feminine mentioned before (in this case, the garden/paradise)

steps.m: to turn over or change from one state to another

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added ان at the start to show something changes by itself instead of being changed by someone else

steps.m: a place or state of return/change
steps.n: Changed to a noun showing the place or result of the action

steps.m: a place of return (as the object in this sentence)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being described in the phrase 'I will surely find better than it as a place of return'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is something that happened in the past. This word shows what his companion (صَاحِبُهُ) did in response to him.
The و changes to ا because it's between two fathas, making the word easier to say
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or purpose. It connects to the word صَاحِبُهُۥ (his companion) to show who is being spoken to.
Added the pronoun 'him' to show who is being referred to. This connects to صَاحِبُهُۥ (his companion) who is speaking in this part of the conversation.
Root letters ص-ح-ب turned into 'companion' by adding vowels. This is the person doing the speaking in this verse.

steps.m: his companion (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added هُۥ to show possession - that this is his companion
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added the pronoun 'he' (هُوَ). This word is the subject of يُحَاوِرُ (he argues/debates) in this ayah.

steps.m: to go back and forth, to discuss
to have a discussion with someone
steps.n: Added alif to show back-and-forth conversation between two people
Added يُ to show this is happening right now, and changed middle vowel to ِ

steps.m: he is having a discussion with him (subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: Added هُۥٓ to show who he's talking with. This connects to the person mentioned earlier who 'said' (قَالَ)
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
The أَ at the beginning makes this a question directed at the companion who is speaking in disbelief about his Creator (اللَّهِ)
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to what they describe, like saying 'the person who ran' in English
Adding ال (the) turns it from 'who/which' to 'the one who/which', making it point to something specific

steps.m: by/with the one who/which (connects to خَلَقَكَ)
steps.n: The بِ at the beginning shows how or by what means something happened. Here it connects to the word خَلَقَكَ (created you) to show 'by/with the one who created you'
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to form the past tense verb meaning 'he created'
Added كَ at the end to show that 'you' are what was created. This word is connected to الَّذِى (the one who) in the previous part of the verse, referring to Allah as the creator.
This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from. In this ayah, it shows that humans were created from dust.
This root relates to dust and earth
Root letters got vowels (u-a) and alif added to make it mean 'actual dust/soil'

steps.m: dust/soil (being acted upon in the sentence)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is the object being created from (مِن تُرَابٍ = from dust)

steps.m: then (after a gap in time) (connects سَوَّىٰكَ with خَلَقَكَ)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows one thing happened after another with a time gap in between. In this ayah, it connects how Allah created you (خَلَقَكَ) first, then after some time, made you into a complete person (سَوَّىٰكَ).

steps.m: from (shows that تُرَابٍ 'dust/soil' is the source/origin)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows the starting point or source of something. In this ayah, it shows that Allah created the person from/out of dust/soil.
Connected the root letters and added ة to make it mean a single drop
Added shadda on the ن to emphasize the word since it comes after another word ending in ن

steps.m: from a drop (of fluid) (object of مِن)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this is the object of the word مِن (from) in the ayah

Added damma (ُ) and fatha with shadda (َّ) to show this is a connecting word that joins events with a time gap between them. In this ayah, it connects two stages of creation: from dust, then from a drop.
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show intensive meaning
Added ى at the end to make it a complete past tense verb

steps.m: he fashioned you completely (object of خَلَقَكَ - created you)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show that 'you' are the object being fashioned. This connects to the earlier verb خَلَقَكَ (created you) in the verse.
Added vowels to make the basic noun form meaning 'man'

steps.m: a man (object of سَوَّىٰكَ - He fashioned you)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence. This word is receiving the action of 'fashioning' (سَوَّىٰكَ)
This is the basic form of the word that shows contrast between ideas
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added alif to strengthen the meaning further
Added نَّا and the small alif (۠) at the end to show this means 'I' with emphasis

This is the basic form of the word meaning 'he'. It's used to refer to someone or something male that was mentioned before. Here it refers to Allah, as shown by the word ٱللَّهُ (Allah) that comes right after it.
Added the vowel marks ُ (damma) and َ (fatha) to show how to pronounce the word correctly as 'huwa'
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Added fatha on first letter and doubled the middle letter to show complete mastery
Added kasra before ي and the ي shows possession. This word refers back to اللَّهُ mentioned earlier in the ayah.
This is a negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'
Added و with fatha at the start to mean 'and', connecting this negation to the previous statement 'But He is Allah, my Lord'
Added elongation mark (آ) to emphasize the negation. This word negates the verb that follows it (أُشْرِكُ - associate partners)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he joined' to 'I join' by adding أُ at the start to mean 'I', and changing the vowels inside the word
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: lord (one who nurtures and cares completely)
steps.n: The verb 'he cared for' becomes 'lord' by doubling the middle letter ب to show complete and perfect care/nurturing
The ي at the end means 'my'
The letter ب (meaning 'by/with') was added to the front of 'my Lord', making it 'by my Lord' - showing what we don't worship alongside Allah
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make it a noun meaning 'one'
Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb أُشْرِكُ (associate). The speaker is saying 'I do not associate anyone with my Lord.'
This is the base conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. The آ is just a spelling variation of ا.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
This is a time particle that connects two events in the past. Here it introduces what should have been said when entering the garden.
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: you entered (addressing the owner of جَنَّتَكَ - your garden)
steps.n: Changed تَ at the end shows this is talking to someone (you) instead of about someone (he). This word is part of a conversation where someone is talking to the owner of a garden.

steps.m: to cover or hide (like how gardens are hidden by trees)

steps.n: Root letters got short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make a noun meaning 'covered place'
Adding ة and doubling ن makes it mean 'a garden' - a covered place with plants

steps.m: your garden (object of دَخَلْتَ - you entered)
steps.n: Added كَ to show it belongs to the person being spoken to. This garden is what was entered in the phrase دَخَلْتَ (you entered).
This root carries the basic meaning of speaking or saying something
Added basic past tense vowels to make it mean 'he said'
The middle weak letter و drops out and the first vowel changes to damma to compensate
Added تَ at the end to change 'he said' to 'you said'

In this ayah, مَا means 'whatever' and is followed by شَآءَ (willed/wished), forming the phrase 'whatever Allah willed.' This is a common Islamic phrase expressing that everything happens by Allah's will.
Added basic past tense vowels to show this is about one male person doing this in the past
The weak letter ي changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with ي in the middle
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added the u-mark at the end to show it's the subject
Added 'the' to make it specific
This is the basic negative particle that negates words after it. Here it negates قُوَّةَ (strength/power), creating the meaning 'there is no power'
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Root meaning 'strong' changed to a noun by adding damma and doubling the middle letter
Added ة to make it into a specific instance of power

steps.m: power/strength (object of لَا - there is no)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of لَا (there is no)
This is a basic exception word that works with the لَا (no/not) before it to say 'except'. Together they mean 'there is no [something] except...'
This is the unique name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning 'divine' but has special spelling and meaning as God's proper name.
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is being done 'by' or 'with' Allah's power. Added ِ at the end to connect it to the previous word. The ٱ is just a different way of writing the same ال.

steps.m: if (introduces condition for تَرَنِ 'you see me')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects 'if you see me' with what follows. The kasra (إِ) is added to show it's the conditional 'if' rather than other meanings of إن.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: you see (addressing the owner of جَنَّتَكَ)
steps.n: Changes from 'he saw' to 'you see' by adding تَ at the start. Ends with ى because it's a special seeing/vision word

steps.m: you see me (the poor man speaking to the garden owner)
steps.n: Adds object pronoun suffix نِ meaning 'me'
This is one of the basic pronouns in Arabic that means 'I'. It's used when you want to emphasize that you are the one doing something.
The small mark (۠) is added on top to show this is a special way of writing the word in the Quran. It doesn't change the meaning, just helps with proper reading.
Root letters connected with fatha vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'became few'

steps.m: less (than you in wealth and children)
steps.n: Added أَ at the start to change 'became little' into 'having less than someone else' - like saying 'I have less than you'
Basic preposition meaning 'from' - shows where something starts or comes from

steps.m: from you (connected to أَقَلَّ - less than)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this is happening to 'you'. This word connects to أَقَلَّ (less than) in the ayah to mean 'less than you'.
The و changes to ا because it's in the middle of the word. Added fatha (َ) to make it a noun meaning wealth.
Added ًا to show this word is being acted upon - it's what is 'less' in the phrase 'less than you in wealth'
Adding vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
Added tanween alif to show this is an indefinite object. This word is one of the objects of تَرَنِ (you see me) in this ayah, along with مَالًا (wealth).
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
The ى at the end makes this a special 'hoping' verb

steps.m: so perhaps/so it may be (followed by رَبِّي - my Lord)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence connected to what came before

steps.m: so perhaps/so it may be (followed by رَبِّي - my Lord)
steps.n: Added fatHa mark to show correct pronunciation
The doubled ب with shadda shows this is someone who nurtures and develops others completely and perfectly

steps.m: my Lord (subject of يُؤْتِيَنِ - may give me)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show this is my Lord. This word is the subject of the verb يُؤْتِيَنِ (may give me) in this ayah, showing what my Lord might do.

steps.m: my Lord (subject of يُؤْتِيَنِ - may give me)
steps.n: Added elongation mark (ٓ) after ى to show extended pronunciation at end of word

steps.m: that (connects to يُؤْتِيَنِ - he will give me)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it connects 'perhaps my Lord' with 'he will give me', showing what might happen.
Connected the root letters together
Added يُ at start to show 'he' does the action. The hamza gets a sukun and the middle letter gets a kasra in this form.
Added نِ at the end to show 'me' as the receiver of the action. The fatha (َ) connects it smoothly to the word.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'good'

steps.m: something good (object of يُؤْتِيَنِ - give me)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is the thing being given. In this ayah, the person is asking Allah to give them something good in return.
This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it's made of. It has no root because it's a primitive word.
The shadda appears because this word comes after a voweled letter, making the م sound stronger. This is a common pronunciation rule in Arabic.

steps.m: to cover or hide (like how a garden's canopy covers the ground)

steps.n: Root means 'to cover' - added ة to make it a noun meaning 'garden' (because gardens have covering trees)
The -i ending shows this garden belongs to someone or comes after a word like 'in' or 'of'

steps.m: your garden (after the word مِّن meaning 'from/than')
steps.n: The word 'garden' got the ending كَ added to show it belongs to 'you' (if talking to one man)
Root letters connected with sukoon and kasra marks to make a command 'send!'
Added يُ at the beginning to show this is happening in the present/future
Added fatha at the end to show this is in the subjunctive mood
Added و at beginning to connect this action with the previous one (giving better than your garden)
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of or over something else
The ى changes to ي to prepare for attaching the next part

steps.m: upon it/her (referring to جَنَّتِكَ - your garden)
steps.n: Added هَا which refers back to the garden (جَنَّتِكَ) mentioned earlier in the verse. The هَا is used because 'garden' is feminine in Arabic.
Added حُ and ان to make it mean 'something that was calculated/measured' - like a punishment that was carefully planned

steps.m: a calculated punishment (that Allah will send)
steps.n: The ًا (an) ending was added to show this is the object - the thing being sent as punishment
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else
Added shadda to show the م is pronounced strongly because it combines with ال of the next word (السماء)
Added a fatha at the end because it connects to the next word that starts with ال (the), making it mean 'from the' instead of just 'from'
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high up
Adds ال to make it specific/definite, with shadda on س
Added kasra at the end because it comes after مِنَ (from) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added ت at start to show a female is doing the action

steps.m: and so it will become (result of يُؤْتِيَنِ - he gives me)
steps.n: Added فَ at start to show this happens as a result of the previous action. The final fatḥa shows it's connected to the previous verb.
Adding vowels to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'to climb up'
Adding ي changes this from a verb (to climb) into a noun meaning the ground that is climbed upon

steps.m: bare/smooth ground (object of فَتُصْبِحَ - it becomes)
steps.n: Added tanween (ًا) to show this word is the object of the verb فَتُصْبِحَ (it becomes)
Adding fatha marks creates the simple past tense verb meaning 'he made slippery'
Added tanween alif (ًا) to make this an adjective describing the ground (صَعِيدًا). Both words match in their endings because they go together.
Basic particle form without any vowel marks

steps.m: or (connecting يُصْبِحَ with what came before)
steps.n: Added fatha on أ and sukun on و to show correct pronunciation. In this ayah, it connects two possibilities about what might happen to the garden's water.
Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end
The root letters change to form the basic noun 'water'. The و changes to ا and a hamza (ء) is added at the end.
The alif is written as آ when it comes at the beginning of a word or after a letter with a vowel
The hamza changes to ؤ and gets a damma (ُ) to show it's doing the action in the sentence
Added ها to show the water belongs to something (feminine). This word is the subject of يُصْبِحَ (becomes/will become) in this ayah.

steps.m: to sink deep, to go down into the earth

steps.n: Root letters got vowel marks (َ and ْ) to make a noun meaning 'depth' from the action of sinking
Added tanween (two damma marks) to make it describe how something becomes - in this case how the water becomes sunken deep
This is a particle that makes something impossible in the future. Here it negates the verb تَسْتَطِيعَ (you will be able to), making it mean 'you will never be able to'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of what came before - the water becoming sunken

steps.m: to follow willingly, to be capable

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Adding است shows someone is trying to do something for themselves
Adding ا after ط changes it to show ability/capability
Changed to present tense with تَ prefix for 'you' and vowel pattern change
The fatha ending shows this is in the subjunctive mood, used after certain particles
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced

steps.m: for him/it (referring to مَآؤُهَا - its water)
steps.n: Added هُ which means 'him/it'. Here it refers back to the water (مَآؤُهَا) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from a verb (action) to a noun (thing/concept)
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb تَسْتَطِيعَ (you will be able to)

steps.m: to surround or protect something by enclosing it

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to passive voice by adding أُ at start and changing vowels. This shows something was done to it rather than it doing something.

steps.m: and it was surrounded (referring to his property being destroyed)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to what happened before in the story - his property being destroyed
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun form
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by something
Added pronoun 'his' to show who owns the fruit
Added preposition بِ to show this is what was surrounded/encompassed in أُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ (his fruit was surrounded)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to show entering into a state or condition

steps.m: and so he became (connected to أُحِيطَ - was surrounded)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened as a result of his garden being surrounded
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows the action is done multiple times or with intensity

steps.m: he turns/flips over repeatedly (his hands in grief)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start, changing middle vowel to kasra, and adding damma at end. In this ayah, he is turning over his hands in regret over his lost garden.

steps.m: palm of hand, to turn over

steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the middle letter 'f' to make the word for 'palm'
Adding ي at the end makes it mean 'two' of something (dual form)
Adds possession marker 'his' to show these are his palms that he is turning over in regret
Adds a small i-sound (kasra) at the end because it comes after the word 'turns over'

steps.m: upon/on/over (preposition showing عُرُوشِهَا is above/on top)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something is physically or metaphorically above something else. Here it shows the garden's trellises (عُرُوشِهَا) have collapsed.

steps.m: upon/on/over (preposition showing عُرُوشِهَا is above/on top)
steps.n: Added an alif mark (ىٰ) at the end to show proper pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: that which (referring to what he spent)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun used to refer to the money/wealth that was spent. In this ayah, it connects to أَنفَقَ (he spent) to mean 'that which he spent'.

steps.m: that which (referring to what he spent)
steps.n: The alif is written with a long vowel mark (آ) instead of a regular alif (ا). This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning or pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning to show causative meaning - making something be spent. This word relates to مَآ (what) in the ayah, showing what was spent on.
Basic preposition meaning 'in' with kasra vowel mark added
Added ها at the end to show this means 'in it' where 'it' refers to something feminine (in this case, his garden)
Added vowel marks to the basic feminine pronoun. This pronoun refers to the garden/property mentioned earlier, which is feminine in Arabic.
Added و at the beginning to connect this clause with the previous one, showing this is part of a sequence of events happening to the garden.
Changes from verb 'to be empty' to adjective meaning 'empty'

This word describes the state of the garden (جنة which is feminine). The ة makes it feminine to match what it's describing, and the tanwin (ٌ) shows it's indefinite - meaning completely or utterly ruined.
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of or above something else. In this ayah, it shows his regret upon/about what he spent.
The alif at the end is written with a special mark (ىٰ) to show it's a long 'aa' sound. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to build or raise structures (especially roofs or trellises)

steps.n: Changes from the root word to plural form by adding 'u' sounds (ُ) and 'waw' (و) to show multiple roofs/structures
Adds ِ because it comes after the word عَلَىٰ (upon/over)
Adds هَا to show these structures belong to the garden mentioned earlier which is now collapsed
Connected the root letters together. The middle letter و changes to ا when we add the vowels.
Changes from past tense (he said) to present tense (he says) by adding يَ at the start

steps.m: and he says/is saying (while looking at his ruined garden)
steps.n: The وَ connects this with what happened before - he's speaking while looking at his destroyed garden
This is the base word expressing a wish or desire
Added يٰ at the beginning to show strong emotion or calling out
Added نِى at the end to show that 'I' am the one wishing. This makes it personal.

steps.m: not (negates أُشْرِكْ 'associate' and makes it past)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of the verb after it (أُشْرِكْ) in two ways: 1) makes it negative ('did not') and 2) changes it from present to past tense. Like saying 'I did not associate' instead of 'I associate'.

steps.m: to join or associate things together

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Added أ at the beginning to show causing something to happen
Changed to passive voice - shows something was done to the subject

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture completely
one who cares for/nurtures completely (subject of يَقُولُ)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this describes someone who does this action perfectly and completely

steps.m: lord (ready to be connected to something)
steps.n: Adding kasra prepares the word for possession/annexation
Adding the possessive pronoun ي makes it 'my lord'
The بِ prefix shows how the action of 'associating partners' (أُشْرِكْ) is done. The long ي (ىٓ) at the end shows emphasis.
Connected the letters with fatha to make it a noun
Adding tanwin alif shows this word is receiving the action of 'associate' in the phrase 'I did not associate anyone with my Lord'
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: fatha on ل and sukun on م. This particle negates verbs in the past.

steps.m: and not (in the past) (connects to يَنصُرُونَهُۥ)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what comes after it. This connects to the verb يَنصُرُونَهُۥ (they help him) in this ayah.
Changed from basic root letters to a noun form by adding the vowel marks ُ and ْ
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense and ُ at end to show it's doing the action
Changed يَ to تَ to show feminine present tense
After لَمْ (not), the word changes to its shortened form by dropping the final ُ and و
Added fatha to show basic meaning of direction or purpose
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added pronoun هُ to show who it's for
Added elongation mark ۥ to show proper pronunciation
Added kasra under first letter to make it into a noun. The hamza (ء) naturally appears between the letters due to pronunciation rules.
Adds -ah ending to make it mean 'a specific group' instead of just 'group' in general

steps.m: a group/party (subject of يَنصُرُونَ - they help/support)
steps.n: The double-damma shows this is the subject doing the action of helping/supporting in the verse
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense form
Adds ي at start and changes middle vowel to show ongoing/present action instead of past
Added ون suffix to make plural masculine (they)

steps.m: they help/are helping him (referring to the one who had no group/company to help him)
steps.n: Added pronoun هُۥ meaning 'him' as the object. This describes the situation of the owner of the garden who had no group (فِئَةٌ) to help him against Allah's punishment.

steps.m: from (connects دُونِ with what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects to دُونِ (besides/other than) to show separation or distance from something.
Adding damma (ُ) changes the word into a common preposition meaning 'without' or 'besides'
Added kasra (ِ) to show this word is connected to Allah (اللَّهِ) that comes after it
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'

Added ال (the) at the beginning, doubled the ل with shadda, and added kasra at the end to show this word's connection to other words in the sentence. This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam.
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context. Here it means 'not' because it's negating كَانَ مُنتَصِرًا (was victorious) in the ayah.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. The full meaning is 'and he was not victorious' in the context of this ayah.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form before the weak letter changes

The middle letter و changes to ا because it's in the middle of the word. This is a common change in Arabic that makes the word easier to say. This word helps describe مُنتَصِرًا (victorious) by showing when it happened.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and ت after ن to show someone helping themselves or becoming victorious

steps.m: being victorious (describing the state of كَانَ)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes how something was, connected to the word كَانَ (was) in the ayah
The word keeps its meaning but the diacritical marks (small marks above/below letters) are simplified, while still showing it describes a state or condition
This is the base demonstrative particle that points to a nearby location
Adding لِ changes the location from nearby to farther away

steps.m: there, in that place or situation (pointing to what was just mentioned)
steps.n: Adding كَ makes it point specifically to something mentioned before - in this case, pointing to the situation just described in the previous ayah

steps.m: to be close to or protect

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed from a past tense verb (he protected) into a noun meaning the act itself (protection) by adding an alif and taa at the end
Added ال for definiteness and ُ to show this is the subject. The word follows هُنَالِكَ (there is) in the ayah.
This is the base word الله (Allah) without any additions. It's a unique word that doesn't come from a three-letter root.
Added kasra (ِ) under the first ل to show this means 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: for/to Allah (belongs to ٱلْوَلٰيَةُ - the authority)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is connected to ٱلْوَلٰيَةُ (authority) in the ayah, meaning 'authority belongs to Allah'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
The middle letter is doubled to add emphasis and make it a noun
Added ال to make it specific (the truth)
Added kasra at the end because it follows the word لِلَّهِ (to/for Allah)

steps.m: he/it (referring to الحَقِّ - the Truth/True One)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun used to refer back to الحَقِّ (the Truth/True One) mentioned earlier in the ayah. The هُ shows it's masculine singular, and the وَ makes it independent (not attached to another word).
Changes from root meaning into noun meaning 'good'
Adding double dammah makes it an indefinite noun in the nominative case
Changed from action (to return/give back) to the thing given back (reward) using the فَعَال pattern

steps.m: as a reward (describes how something is better)
steps.n: Added ًا (tanween) to make it 'as a reward' - shows it's describing something
Changes the word into a noun meaning 'good' or 'better'
Added tanween damma (ٌ) to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added و with fatha (وَ) at the beginning to mean 'and'
Added ُ on first letter and ْ on middle letter to change it from an action into a thing - the result that comes after something
Added ًا at the end to make it a direct object (something receiving the action) - it's what is 'better' in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make basic past tense form
Changed vowels to make it a command (telling someone to do something)
Added helping alif at beginning to make pronunciation easier

steps.m: and present! (a parable) (command to Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Added و (and) to connect with previous statement. The word here means to present or give an example, as shown by the context مَّثَلَ (parable) that follows.
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: for them (connected to وَٱضْرِبْ - and strike/present)
steps.n: Added هُم at the end to show who the preposition is directed to. This connects to the command وَٱضْرِبْ (and strike/present), showing what should be presented to them.

steps.m: to be similar to something or to give an example

steps.n: Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
The م gets doubled (shadda) because it comes right after a word ending in a sukun - this is a common rule in Arabic where letters merge together when one has sukun
This root represents the basic concept of living or being alive
Root meaning 'live' becomes the noun 'life' by adding و and ة - like changing 'to live' into 'life'
Adding ال makes it specific - like changing 'life' to 'the life'
Adding ِ shows this word is being described by another word
Root meaning 'near' became a word for 'worldly life' since it's closer to us than the afterlife
Added 'the' to specify we're talking about this particular life (the worldly life)
Added fatha mark to show this word is the object of something
The root letters change to form the basic noun 'water'. The و and ه combine into a hamza (ء) at the end.
Adding vowel marks and extending the alif for emphasis
Adding the two dots below (tanween) shows this is 'some water' that something is being done to
Adding كَ at the beginning makes this a comparison. This water is being compared to worldly life in the parable.

steps.m: to cause to descend, send down
steps.n: Adding أَ at the beginning makes the meaning 'cause someone/something to do the action'. Here it changes 'descend' to 'cause to descend/send down'.
Adding َ (fatha) at the end shows this happened in the past
Added نٰ to show 'we' are doing the action
Added هُ to show what was sent down (it)
This is the basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts from, like saying 'from' in English
Added a fatha (َ) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word السَّمَآءِ (the sky). This is like a bridge between words to make them easier to say together.
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high/elevated
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda
Added kasra because it comes after the word مِنَ (from) in the ayah

steps.m: to mix or combine things together

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: it mixed with itself (referring to نَبَاتُ - the plants)
steps.n: Added ٱ at start and ت after first letter to show the mixing happened by itself, with sukun on first letter to help pronunciation

steps.m: and so it mixed with itself (referring to نَبَاتُ - the plants)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this mixing was a result of what happened before (the water being sent down)
Basic preposition that shows how something is done

steps.m: with it (refers to مَآء/water mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added pronoun هِ (it) to show what the preposition connects to. Here it refers back to the water (مَآء) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added small alif (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation guide to show the هِ should be clearly pronounced

steps.m: to grow or sprout from the ground

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun by adding alif after second letter and damma at the end. This word describes what mixes with the water mentioned earlier in the ayah.
The root letters form the basic word 'earth'. Here it shows the earth owns/has the plants growing in it. Like saying 'the earth's plants'.
The ِ at the end shows this word is being used after a preposition or is possessed by something
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
The أ prefix changes the meaning from literally entering morning to metaphorically entering a state or condition. The middle letter gets a sukun.

steps.m: and so it became (describing how the plants mentioned before became dried up)
steps.n: The فَ shows this is a result of what happened in the previous ayah where Allah mentions sending down rain that mixed with plants

steps.m: to break or crush into small pieces

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters with vowel changes to show the complete state of being broken up

steps.m: as broken up pieces (object of فَأَصْبَحَ - then it became)
steps.n: Added tanween fatHa to show this is the object describing what something became

steps.m: to scatter or spread things in the wind

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb

steps.m: she/it scatters (subject is الرِّيٰحُ - the winds)
steps.n: Changed to present tense feminine form since the subject is الرِّيٰحُ (the winds), which is feminine in Arabic

steps.m: she/it scatters it (the winds scatter it)
steps.n: Added هُ to show what is being scattered. This refers back to هَشِيمًا (dry stalks) mentioned earlier in the verse.
The middle و changes to ي and kasra added to make it mean the thing that moves through air
Changed from single wind to plural winds by adding alif and making it رِيَاح
Added ال to make it specific/definite winds
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: to be or exist in a state/condition

steps.n: The middle weak letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in past tense

steps.m: and he/it was (connected to فَأَصْبَحَ - then it became)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement about the plants becoming dry stubble

steps.m: a deity, one who is worshipped
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Adding 'the' makes it refer to a specific deity rather than any deity
This is the basic form of the preposition showing position or authority over something

Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show this is a complete word. In this ayah, it connects مُّقْتَدِرًا (All-Powerful) with كُلِّ شَىْءٍ (everything), showing Allah's power extends over all things.
The doubled ل with damma makes it mean 'everything' or 'all' instead of just the action of containing
The kasra shows this word is connected to شَىْءٍ (thing) that comes after it, forming the phrase 'every thing'
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to make it a noun. This word follows كُلِّ (every/all) in this ayah.
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by كُلِّ (every/all) that comes before it.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Active participle from Form VIII (اقتدر) meaning 'one who takes/exercises power' - shows someone doing the action
The م is emphasized with a shadda (doubled), making the word stronger in meaning
Adds -an ending to describe Allah as All-Powerful in this specific context
Forms the basic noun meaning 'wealth'. The ٌ (tanween) shows it's indefinite.
Adding ال makes it definite ('the wealth'). The ُ shows it's the subject doing something in the sentence - here it is what serves as the adornment (زِينَةُ) of worldly life.
The root meaning 'to build' transforms into the word for 'son', showing how sons help build and continue the family. This word is described as one of life's decorations (زِينَةُ).
Special plural form of 'son' (بن) that adds ون to make it mean 'sons'. Like adding 's' in English but using ون instead.
Adding ال makes it refer to sons in general as one of the adornments of worldly life
Added و to connect with المال (wealth) as they are both described as worldly adornments
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed the vowel pattern and added ة to create a noun meaning the result of beautifying

steps.m: adornment (subject of الْمَالُ وَالْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ - wealth and children are adornment)
steps.n: Added damma to show this word is the predicate (what is being said about) wealth and children in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The final ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added و and ة to make it a noun meaning the complete concept of life
Added ٱلْ to make it definite ('the life'). The ِ at the end shows it's connected to the word الدُّنْيَا (worldly) that comes after it, forming the phrase 'the worldly life'.
Forms a feminine adjective meaning 'lower' or 'closer' which came to refer to this worldly life as opposed to the afterlife
Adding ال makes it definite. The د doubles (gets shadda) because it's a 'sun letter' that assimilates with ال. In this ayah, it's describing 'the life' (ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ) mentioned earlier.
Forms active participle meaning 'something that remains/lasts'. This word is part of the subject of خير (better) in this ayah, describing the good deeds that last.
Makes the word feminine plural to describe multiple things that remain/last
Adds ال to specify these particular remaining things, and ُ to show this is the subject (doer). In this ayah, it's specifically referring to good deeds that last.

steps.m: and the remaining/lasting things (subject of خَيْرٌ)
steps.n: Adds و to connect this to the previous phrase about worldly wealth and children, contrasting temporary worldly things with lasting good deeds
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Forms active participle meaning 'one who is righteous'. This word is an adjective describing الباقيات (the enduring things) and matches it in case (nominative), gender (feminine), and being plural.
Makes word feminine plural, shifts meaning to 'righteous deeds'. This word is an adjective describing الباقيات (the enduring things) and matches its case.
Makes the word definite
Changed alif to waslah form, added damma ending to show it's the subject, and elongated the alif in ات to match Uthmani script. This word is the subject of خَيْرٌ (better) in this ayah.
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'good/goodness'
Adding double dammah makes it indefinite and shows it's the subject
Starting with the basic concept of being present or near

Adding the vowel marks changes this from a basic concept into a preposition that shows location or presence. In this ayah, it comes before رَبِّكَ (your Lord) to show something is 'with' or 'at' your Lord.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, and guide with authority
Lord (one who nurtures and guides with complete authority)
steps.n: Doubling the middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and guides perfectly

steps.m: Lord (being described or connected to something)
steps.n: Adding kasra shows this word is being connected to something that comes after it
Adding كَ shows this is your Lord - the one who nurtures and guides you
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
This pattern turns the action into a noun meaning the result of that action

steps.m: as a reward (explaining how something is better in this ayah)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is explaining in what way the righteous deeds (ٱلْبٰقِيٰتُ ٱلصَّالِحٰتُ) are better (خَيْرٌ)
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create comparative/superlative form. This word is being compared to زِينَةُ (adornment) in the ayah.
Added وَ (and) at beginning and ٌ (dammatan) at end to show this is indefinite and nominative case, connecting it to the previous statement about worldly adornments
Adding vowel marks to make the word pronounceable
Changed from verb 'hoped' to noun 'hope' and added tanween to show it's being compared
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a proper word
Added و with fatha to connect this word to what comes after it
Added fatha at the end to show this word is connected to the verb نُسَيِّرُ (we will move) that comes after it

steps.m: to move or travel along a path

steps.n: The doubled middle letter makes it mean 'causing something to move' instead of just moving
Added نُ at the start to show 'we' are doing the action, and ُ at the end to show it's happening now or in the future. This word is acting on الْجِبَالَ (the mountains) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to form the basic word for a single mountain
Changes the vowel pattern and adds alif to make it plural, showing many mountains
Adds ال to specify these particular mountains being moved on that day

steps.m: the mountains (object of نُسَيِّرُ - we move)
steps.n: Adds fatha at the end to show the mountains are the object (the thing being moved)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' and removed أ because it comes between two vowels. This word is addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in this ayah.

steps.m: and you see (addressing the Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this with the previous part of the ayah
Basic root letters got vowel marks to make the word 'earth/land'
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific earth rather than any land

steps.m: the earth (as object of تَرَى/you see)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being seen in the phrase 'you see the earth'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form to describe something that is visible/apparent
Makes the word feminine to describe a feminine noun
Changed ending to show this describes الْأَرْضَ (the earth) which is being seen in the verse وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ (and you will see the earth)
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it past tense
Added نا to show 'we' did the action
Added هُمْ to show who was gathered
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this action with what came before
This is a basic negative particle that makes present-tense verbs refer to the past. It's used with the verb نُغَادِرْ (leave/abandon) in this ayah to say 'did not leave'.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of or after something else. Here it connects to what happened before in the ayah.
Added basic past tense form by connecting the letters with fatha marks
Adding alif makes the action more deliberate and complete
Added نُ at start to show 'we' are doing the action, changed middle vowel to kasra, and added sukun at end for jussive mood after لَمْ
Basic preposition meaning 'from'

steps.m: from them (referring to those gathered on that Day)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to. This connects to حَشَرْنٰهُمْ (We gathered them) in the ayah.

steps.m: to be one, unique, or alone

steps.n: Connected the root letters and added fatha marks to create a noun meaning 'one' or 'someone'

steps.m: anyone (object of نُغَادِرْ - we left out)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this word is receiving the action of the verb نُغَادِرْ (we left out). The meaning becomes 'anyone' in the context of 'we did not leave out anyone'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than something doing it
Added وا to show many people were presented/displayed

steps.m: and they were presented/displayed (before رَبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with what came before it. This word shows people being presented before their Lord.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or upon something else. In this ayah, it connects to رَبِّكَ (your Lord) to show they are presented 'before' or 'to' your Lord.
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show this is the full form of the word. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and cares for others perfectly
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this Lord belongs to or is connected to something
Adding كَ shows this is your Lord. This whole phrase عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ means 'upon your Lord' or 'before your Lord'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Doubled the middle letter to show intensity and turned it into a noun

steps.m: in rows/lines (describing how they were presented before their Lord)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this describes how the action happened. This word explains how people were arranged when they were presented before their Lord in the ayah.
This is a basic particle that shows something is certain or has already happened, like saying 'indeed' or 'already' in English
Adding لَ at the beginning makes the meaning stronger, like adding 'certainly' to 'indeed'
The shadda (ّ) on the ل makes the emphasis even stronger, like saying 'most certainly indeed'
Added fatha vowels and changed middle letter ي to ا, which is common for this type of word in past tense
Changed from past tense 'he came' to command form 'come!' by changing the middle vowel
Added past tense plural 'you' ending
Added و to connect to an object pronoun that will follow
Added نَا at the end to show the action was done to 'us'
Starting with the basic comparison particle ك with its core meaning of comparing things

steps.m: just as/like (connects to خَلَقْنٰكُمْ - We created you)
steps.n: Added ما to make the comparison more specific and connect it to what follows. This word introduces how Allah created humans the first time, comparing it to how He will recreate them.

steps.m: to create, make, or bring into existence

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added sukun (ْ) to prepare for attaching more parts
Added نا to show multiple people did the action
Added كم to show the object (you all) receiving the action
Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters
The shadda (ّ) on و makes the meaning stronger and more definite. This word appears in the phrase أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ meaning 'first time'
Adding fatha (َ) makes it a past tense verb
Added ة to make it into a noun meaning 'one instance'
Added tanween kasra to show indefiniteness

steps.m: rather/but (contradicting their claim in كَمَا خَلَقْنٰكُمْ)
steps.n: This is a particle that introduces a contradiction to what was said before. Here it contradicts their claim about being created like the first time, leading into Allah mentioning what they actually claimed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added sukun to prepare for adding suffix
Added تُمْ to show that multiple people are being addressed
This is the first particle أَن which introduces a new clause and means 'that'
Added لَن which is used to negate future actions
The ن from أَن combines with the ل from لَن to create a doubled ل (shown by the ّ mark). This is just a pronunciation change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to make something into something else, or to put something somewhere

steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added نَ prefix to indicate 'we' as the subject in present tense
Added fatha at end to show this is in the subjunctive mood (like 'that we make')
The ن gets a shadda because it comes after لَ in أَلَّن (that not) in the ayah
Starting with the basic particle ل that shows direction or purpose, adding fatha for pronunciation
Added كم to show this applies to multiple people being addressed

steps.m: to promise, appoint, or set a time

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to promise' into noun meaning 'place/time of promise'
Makes the word indefinite and marks it as the object in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than something doing it
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from verb 'to write' to noun 'book' using the فِعَال pattern. This word is the subject of وُضِعَ (was placed/laid).
Made definite by adding ال prefix
Added damma to show this is the subject. Also replaced 'aa' with proper long alif (ٰ)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of a word.
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' and removed أ because it combines with the ت prefix
Added فَ to show this happens after the previous action (placing of the book). This verb's object is مُجْرِمِينَ (wrongdoers) in the ayah.
Adds مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show who does the action (like changing 'crime' to 'criminal')
Adds ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple criminals. These criminals are being seen (object of ترى/you see) in this verse.
Adds ال at the start to point to specific criminals, not just any criminals. These are the specific criminals being seen in the verse.

steps.m: to be anxious/fearful about something bad happening

steps.n: Adds مُ at the start to show who does the action, and ِ in the middle to complete the pattern. This makes it describe someone feeling this way.

steps.m: anxious/fearful ones (describing the criminals in the ayah)
steps.n: Adds ين to show this describes multiple people. In this ayah, it's describing how the criminals (المجرمين) are feeling when they see their book of deeds.
Combined two basic words: مِن meaning 'from' and مَا meaning 'what'
When مِن comes before a word starting with م, its ن combines with that م to make a stronger م sound (shown by ّ)
Basic preposition meaning 'in'
Added ه at the end to show what is being referred to (the record book mentioned in the ayah)
Changed vowels to show present tense form. The middle weak letter changes back to و.
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense
Added ونَ at the end to show multiple people doing the action
Added وَ at start to connect this with the previous part of the ayah
This is the basic word for expressing misfortune or distress
Adding ة makes it refer to one specific instance of woe
The ة changes to ت when adding possessive endings
Adding نا shows this woe belongs to or affects us

steps.m: Oh, woe to us! (expressing distress in يَقُولُونَ - they say)
steps.n: Adding يٰ at the beginning makes it into an exclamation of distress. This word appears in what the wrongdoers are saying (يَقُولُونَ) when they see their book of deeds.

steps.m: What is with/what is the matter with
steps.n: This is an interrogative particle used to ask about the reason or state of something
This is the basic demonstrative element that points to something
The alif is replaced with a small alif above the ه to show it's a demonstrative
The ذ is added to make it specifically point to something nearby

steps.m: this (masculine singular) (pointing to الْكِتٰبِ - the book)
steps.n: The ا is added to make it masculine singular. In this ayah, it's pointing to 'the book' (الْكِتٰبِ) that's being discussed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (wrote) to a noun (book) by adding alif
Added ٱلْ to make it definite ('the book') and ِ at the end because it follows a preposition in the ayah. The alif (ا) is written as ٰ when it comes after certain letters.
This is a negative particle that tells us the book does NOT leave anything out. It works with the verb يُغَادِرُ (leaves out) to say what the book doesn't do.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Adding alif makes it mean 'to leave' rather than just 'leaving out'

steps.m: he leaves out (in the context of a book not leaving out anything)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. In this ayah, it describes how the book doesn't leave out any deed, small or large.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (became small) to a describing word (small) by adding ي
Makes the word feminine by adding ة, used when describing feminine nouns

steps.m: a small (thing) (object of لَا يُغَادِرُ - does not leave out)
steps.n: Adds tanween fatha to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb لَا يُغَادِرُ (does not leave out)
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يَظْلِمُ that comes after it, changing its meaning from 'wrongs' to 'does not wrong'
The و connects this negation to what came before, while لا negates the verb يَظْلِمُ that follows
Added vowels and ي to create an adjective describing a strong quality
Added ة to make the word feminine - like changing 'big' to match a feminine noun

steps.m: a big/large thing (feminine, being acted upon)
steps.n: Added tanween (ً) to show this is an indefinite noun being acted upon. In this ayah, it refers to a big/large deed or matter that the book records.

This is a basic word that excludes something from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it's used with أَحْصَىٰهَا (counted it) to say the book doesn't leave anything out EXCEPT that it counted it.
The alif at the end is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but this doesn't change the meaning
This root relates to counting, calculating, or keeping track of things
Added أَ at the beginning to show causing something to happen. The ي at the end changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added the alif khanjariya (ٰ) mark to show the long vowel sound clearly

steps.m: he counted/calculated it (referring to everything in the book)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show what is being counted. In this ayah, it refers to everything written in the book mentioned earlier.
Basic meaning of finding or discovering something
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he found' in the past
Added وا to show multiple people did the action

steps.m: and they found (what they had done present before them)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at beginning to connect with previous statement about the book being placed. This word connects to مَا عَمِلُوا۟ (what they had done) which comes after it.
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb عَمِلُوا (they did/performed). In this context, it means 'what they did' rather than being used as a question word.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action
This is the basic meaning of being physically present somewhere
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who or what is doing the action

steps.m: present (as the thing found by وَجَدُوا)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha at the end to show this is what they found in the verse - it's the object of the verb وَجَدُوا (they found)
This is a basic negative particle that negates the word كَبِيرَةً (big/major) in this ayah
The و connects this negation with the previous negation of صَغِيرَةً (small/minor), showing that neither small nor big things are left out
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense form

steps.m: he wrongs (in relation to رَبُّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Changes past tense to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing vowels, and adding ُ at end. This word relates to رَبُّكَ (your Lord) in the ayah, showing that your Lord does not wrong anyone.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows intensity, indicating complete and ongoing nurturing and care
Adding damma to show this is the doer of the verb يَظْلِمُ (does wrong/oppresses)
Adding 2nd person masculine singular possessive pronoun suffix. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb يظلم (does wrong/oppress).
Connected the letters together with basic vowels to form the noun meaning 'one'
Added tanween alif to show this word is receiving the action of the verb يُغَادِرُ (leaves out/omits). In the ayah, it's saying the book doesn't leave out anyone.

steps.m: when (in the past, referring to the time Allah commanded the angels)
steps.n: This is a time word that points to something that happened in the past. Here it refers to when Allah commanded the angels about Adam.

steps.m: and when (connecting this story to what came before)
steps.n: The و connects this story to previous stories in the Quran, like saying 'and here's another story to remember'
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a special kind of letter that changes in the past tense.

steps.m: we said (connected to وَإِذْ - and when)
steps.n: Changed the vowel to ُ and added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word connects to وَإِذْ (and when) to tell about something that happened in the past.
Forms the basic past tense verb with active meaning
Changed from a verb meaning 'to own/rule' into a noun meaning 'one who carries out commands' (like a messenger)
Changes to make plural: adds alif after first letter, adds hamza, and adds kasra under second-to-last letter
Adds taa marboota to form collective plural noun
Added a kasra (small diagonal line) at the end to show these angels belong to/are connected to something
ال makes the word definite
Adds preposition لِ meaning 'to/for'

steps.m: to prostrate, bow down in worship

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Changed from 'he prostrated' to 'prostrate!' by adding 'u' at start and changing vowels to make it a command
Changed command from singular to plural by adding وا at end and changing initial alif to waṣla form
Changes from verb to noun meaning 'one who does the action'

steps.m: to Adam (the one commanded to be prostrated to)
steps.n: Added لِ to show direction 'to' and َ at the end to show Adam is receiving the action. This connects to the command 'prostrate' (اسْجُدُوا) in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added وا to show many people did the action

steps.m: so they bowed down in worship (in response to ٱسْجُدُوا۟)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this happened as a result of the command to prostrate (ٱسْجُدُوا۟) mentioned earlier in the verse
Basic negating particle that introduces exceptions. In this ayah, it excludes Iblis from those who prostrated.
Adding shadda emphasizes the exception, making it clear and definite
The long alif at the end shows where to pause when reading. This is important because it comes right before mentioning Iblis.

steps.m: Iblis (the name of the chief devil)
steps.n: This is a proper name for Satan, possibly derived from Greek 'diabolos' meaning 'slanderer'. In this ayah, he is being mentioned as the one who refused to bow to Adam.

steps.m: Iblis (as the subject being described in كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because Iblis is the subject being described in the next part of the ayah that says 'he was from among the jinn'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show past tense
The و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Basic preposition showing origin, source, or type. In this ayah, it connects to الْجِنِّ (the jinn) to show that Iblis was from among the jinn.

steps.m: to hide or be hidden from sight

steps.n: The doubled ن (with shadda) shows this is a special type of hidden being
Adding ٱلْ at the beginning points to specific jinn being talked about in this verse

steps.m: of the jinn (part of the phrase كَانَ مِنَ - he was from)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word comes after مِنَ (from) in the phrase 'he was from the jinn'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word describes what Iblis did after being commanded to prostrate.
Added فَ at beginning to show this disobedience was a result of what happened before (refusing to prostrate)
This is a preposition that shows separation or distance from something. In this ayah, it shows Iblis turning away from Allah's command.
Changed from action to thing by adding fatha and sukun marks. This makes it a noun meaning 'command' instead of the action 'to command'.
Added kasra at the end to show this command belongs to his Lord (رَبِّهِ) who is mentioned right after it in the ayah

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop to completion

steps.n: Doubling the ب makes it mean 'one who does a lot of nurturing'
Added kasra before shadda to show this word is owned by something else
Added هِ suffix with elongation marks to show this belongs to 'him'. This word is possessed by أَمْرِ (command) in the phrase أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ (command of his lord).
Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
The ا (alif) was removed and ت got a shadda (ّ) to show doing something for yourself
Changes from past tense (took) to present tense (takes)
Adds 'you/she' marker at start of word
Makes the verb plural (more than one person)
Adds هُ to show who is being taken/adopted
Adds أَ to turn the statement into a yes/no question
Adds فَ to show this question follows from previous statement
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
From 'spread out' to 'offspring' - like how children spread out from parents over generations
Adding ة at the end makes it mean 'a group' instead of just 'offspring'
Added conjunction وَ (and) at beginning to connect to previous phrase
Adds attached pronoun هُ meaning 'his' to indicate possession. This word is the object of the verb تَتَّخِذُونَ (you take) in this ayah.

steps.m: to be close to and protect/support

steps.n: Adding vowels changes it from root to past tense verb
Changed from single 'protector' to plural 'protectors' by adding hamza at start and changing the word pattern to make it plural

steps.m: protectors/allies (as objects of أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُۥ - will you then take)
steps.n: Added fatha at end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb 'take' in the question
Basic preposition showing that Iblis came from or was part of the jinn. In this ayah, it connects Iblis (إِبْلِيسَ) to the jinn (ٱلْجِنِّ) to show his origin.
The meaning shifted from being lower to meaning 'besides' or 'other than'
Adding the damma vowel mark changes it into a word meaning 'besides' or 'other than'

steps.m: besides me/other than me (object of مِن)
steps.n: Adding ى shows this means 'besides me'. This word follows مِن (from/of) in the ayah, showing what is being taken as protectors instead of Allah.

steps.m: they/them (referring to Iblis and his descendants mentioned as ذُرِّيَّتَهُ)
steps.n: Basic pronoun for third person plural masculine. In this ayah, it refers back to Iblis and his descendants who were just mentioned.
Added وَ to mean 'and'. This connects to the next word عَدُوٌّۢ (enemies) to form the complete thought 'and they are enemies'
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose. Here it connects to the word أَوْلِيَآءَ (allies/protectors) in the ayah.
Added كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. In this ayah, it's asking about taking Iblis and his offspring as protectors.
The pattern makes it into a noun describing someone with this quality
Added tanween to show this is referring to any enemy in general, not a specific one
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it express strong disapproval
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word to show who does the action, like changing 'wrong' to 'wrongdoer'
Added ين to show we're talking about many wrongdoers
Added ال (the) and doubled the ظ letter to show we're talking about specific wrongdoers
Added لِ at the beginning to show 'for' or 'to' the wrongdoers
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create a noun meaning the result of exchanging

steps.m: as a replacement (object of بِئْسَ - how wretched)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being criticized by the word بِئْسَ (how wretched) in the ayah
This is a basic negative particle that makes something negative, like adding 'not' in English
Adding shadda (double mark) on م makes the word stronger, like saying 'definitely not' instead of just 'not'
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but this doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding hamza prefix creates causative meaning. The word خَلْقَ is the direct object (accusative case) of the verb أشهد, showing what they were not made to witness.
Adding ت makes it first person singular past tense
Adding shadda makes it mean 'to make someone do the action'
Adding هُمْ shows who received the action - 'them'

steps.m: to create or form something from nothing
